questions.text: How are clouds made?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While heat absorption can lead to the evaporation of water, which is an initial step in the water cycle, cloud formation occurs when water vapor in the atmosphere undergoes cooling and condenses into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals. So, while heat absorption plays a role in the water cycle, it is not the direct cause of cloud formation.

This option is incorrect. Melting ice involves a phase change from solid to liquid, but it does not directly contribute to cloud formation. Cloud formation occurs when water vapor in the atmosphere cools and condenses into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals, usually through the cooling of air containing water vapor.

This option is incorrect. Cloud formation is not solely dependent on temperature; it can occur in a range of temperature conditions. Clouds typically form when warm, moist air rises and cools, causing water vapor to condense into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals. While temperature influences the rate of evaporation and condensation, it is not the sole determinant of cloud formation.

Clouds form when water vapor in the atmosphere cools and condenses into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals. The cooling of air containing water vapor is essential for cloud formation and the cooling of vapors is due to them releasing heat. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: Clouds are formed when water vapors condense to form water droplets (an exothermic process, so heat is released), which form a floating layer in the sky.

questions.text: The living system works in _ condition.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Isochoric processes occur at constant volume. While there may be instances where biological processes occur at constant volume, such as certain cellular processes, many biological systems involve changes in volume. Therefore, this option is not the most general representation of how living systems work.

Isothermal processes occur at constant temperatures. While some biological processes may occur at relatively constant temperatures, many biological systems involve temperature changes due to metabolic activity or environmental factors. Therefore, while some biological processes may be isothermal, it is not a comprehensive description of how living systems work in general. However this is the closest possible answer.

Adiabatic processes occur without the transfer of heat between the system and its surroundings. While some biological processes may operate under conditions with minimal heat exchange, many biological systems do involve heat exchange with the environment. Therefore, while adiabatic processes may apply to certain biological phenomena, they are not universally characteristic of living systems.

Isobaric processes occur at constant pressure. While there may be instances where biological processes occur at constant pressure, such as breathing or blood circulation, many biological systems involve changes in pressure. Therefore, while some biological processes may be isobaric, it is not the most general representation of how living systems work.

questions.explanation: The living system works in isothermal conditions i.e the temperature of the body remains constant due to thermoregulatory mechanisms. This is important for enzyme activity as enzymes can work best at only a specific temperature, the optimum temperature. For humans, the optimum temperature for enzymes is 37°C.

questions.text: Which one is true about Cp and Cv?

options.explanation: This option suggests that Cp is equal to Cv for all substances, which is generally not true. In many cases, Cp is greater than Cv, especially for gases.
This option suggests that none of the options is correct. However, this option is incorrect since option C is correct.

This option suggests that Cp is greater than Cv, which is generally true for many substances, especially gases. The reason for this difference lies in the fact that at constant pressure, the system can perform work on its surroundings by expanding, allowing for additional energy to be stored as work in addition to the internal energy change associated with the temperature change. This results in Cp being greater than Cv.

This option is typically not true. It suggests that Cv is greater than Cp, which is generally not the case. Cp is typically greater than Cv, especially for gases, due to the reasons mentioned earlier.

questions.explanation: CV is the specific heat at constant volume, and CP is the specific heat at constant pressure.When a gas is heated at a constant volume, the work done on the gas increases the internal energy of the system. At constant pressure, when a gas is heated, work is done to overcome the pressure and there is an expansion in the volume with an increase in the internal energy of the system. Therefore, it can be said that Cp is greater than Cv.For an ideal gas,Cp - Cv = R.Thus, it can also be proved mathematically that Cp is greater than Cv.

questions.text: Which of the following has the highest heat of vaporization?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Ethene, also known as ethylene (C2H4), is a hydrocarbon compound. While it does have a heat of vaporization, it is not particularly high compared to other substances. Ethene molecules are held together by relatively weak London dispersion forces, which do not require as much energy to overcome compared to the intermolecular forces present in water.

Nitrogen gas (N2) is a diatomic molecule commonly found in its gaseous state at room temperature and pressure. While nitrogen gas does have a heat of vaporization, it is relatively low compared to many other substances. Nitrogen molecules are held together by relatively weak nitrogen-nitrogen triple bonds, which do not require as much energy to overcome compared to the intermolecular forces present in water.

options.text: All have the same heat of vaporization
options.explanation: This option suggests that all substances have the same heat of vaporization, which is not true. The heat of vaporization varies greatly depending on the substance. Different substances have different intermolecular forces and molecular structures, leading to different amounts of energy required to transition from liquid to gas phase.

Water has the highest heat of vaporization among the options provided. The heat of vaporization of water is relatively high due to the strong hydrogen bonds between water molecules in the liquid phase. These hydrogen bonds require a significant amount of energy to break, resulting in a high heat of vaporization for water.

questions.explanation: The heat of vaporization is defined as the amount of heat required to convert 1g of a liquid into vapors, without any change in temperature. The heat of vaporization depends on the intermolecular forces in a substance. Water molecules have a strong hydrogen bonding, which accounts for their high heat of vaporization at 40.8 kJ/mol.Ethene (ethylene) and nitrogen have heats of vaporization of 13.5 kJ/mole and 2.79 kJ/mol respectively.

questions.text: Which of the following is a unit of heat of vaporization?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A calorie (cal) is a unit of energy commonly used in nutrition and thermodynamics. While it is a valid unit of energy, it is not typically used to express the heat of vaporization. Instead, calories are often used to quantify energy content in food or describe energy changes in chemical reactions.

A kilojoule (kJ) is a unit of energy in the International System of Units (SI), equal to 1000 joules. While kilojoules are commonly used to express energy in various contexts, including heat transfer and thermodynamics, they are not typically used to express the heat of vaporization. Instead, the heat of vaporization is often expressed in kilojoules per mole (kJ/mol) or kilojoules per kilogram (kJ/kg).

The joule (J) is the SI unit of energy and is defined as the work done by a force of one newton acting over a distance of one meter. While the joule is a fundamental unit of energy, it is typically used to express smaller quantities of energy compared to the heat of vaporization. However, the heat of vaporization can indeed be expressed in joules per mole (J/mol) or joules per kilogram (J/kg).

This option suggests a unit of energy per unit mass, specifically calories per gram. While it is a valid unit of energy, it is not commonly used to express the heat of vaporization. The heat of vaporization is usually expressed in terms of energy per mole or energy per unit mass, such as joules per mole (J/mol) or kilojoules per kilogram (kJ/kg), respectively. This option is thus correct.

questions.explanation: The heat of vaporization (ΔHv) is defined as the energy (Q) required to vaporize 1g of a liquid without any change in its temperature. The heat of vaporization formula is:ΔHv = Q/mOf all the given options, only 'calories per gram' fits this definition. Calories, joules, and kilojoules are units of energy.

questions.text: If the reaction is carried out at constant volume and no work is done, then the change in internal energy is equal to which factor?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Enthalpy (H) represents the total heat content of a system at constant pressure. It is related to the internal energy (U) of the system by the equation H=U+PV, where P is pressure and V is volume. At constant volume, enthalpy is not directly relevant.

Work (W) is a form of energy transfer that occurs when a force is applied to an object and it moves in the direction of the force. In this scenario, where the reaction is carried out at constant volume and no work is done, W=0. Therefore, work is not relevant to the change in internal energy.

Entropy (S) is a measure of the disorder or randomness of a system. While entropy plays a crucial role in thermodynamics, it does not directly determine the change in internal energy at constant volume.

Heat (Q) is the transfer of thermal energy between systems at different temperatures. In this scenario, where the reaction is carried out at constant volume and no work is done, the change in internal energy (ΔU) is equal to the heat added or removed (Q) from the system.

questions.explanation: As the first law of thermodynamics is given byΔU = ΔQ - ΔWwhere ΔU is the change in internal energyΔQ is the heat addedand ΔW is the work doneIf the work done on the system is 0 then according to the equation the change in internal energy is equal to heat.ΔU = ΔQ - 0ΔU = ΔQ.

questions.text: Which of these is constant in the adiabatic process?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An adiabatic process is a thermodynamic process during which no energy is transferred as heat across the boundaries of the system. As there is no exchange of heat with surroundings, so total heat of the system remains constant.
In an isochoric process, the volume of the gas remains constant.So, work done is constant
The measure of a system's thermal energy per unit temperature that is unavailable for doing useful work is entropy .It remains constant
Thus, A and C are correct

questions.explanation: In an adiabatic process, the quantity that remains constant is the entropy and total heat.The term "adiabatic" refers to a process in which there is no heat transfer between the system and its surroundings. In such a scenario, the system is thermally isolated, meaning there is no exchange of heat energy.While several properties can change during an adiabatic process, such as pressure, temperature, volume, and internal energy, the entropy of the system remains constant. The entropy is a measure of the system's disorder or randomness.In an adiabatic process, the absence of heat transfer implies that the system's entropy remains constant because entropy change is associated with heat exchange. Therefore, the entropy is a conserved quantity in an adiabatic process.

The adiabatic process is a thermodynamic process in which there is no heat transfer from in or out of the system. An adiabatic process is a reversible process with constant entropy for an ideal gas. The mathematical representation of the adiabatic process is ΔQ=0

questions.text: A thermodynamic system undergoes a process in which its internal energy decreases by 300 J. If at the same time, 120 J of work is done on the system, find the heat lost by the system.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to the First Law of Thermodynamics:Q = ΔU + WAs in the question, the internal energy is decreased by 300J, so ΔU will be -300J. And in physics, work done on the system is taken as negative, so W = -120J.Substituting the values, we get:Q = -300 + (-120) = -300 - 120= -420J.Therefore, the heat lost by the system is -420 J.

questions.text: If a system changes from a state P1V1 to P2V2 by two paths then the quantity which remains unchanged is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Heat (Q) is not a state function. Its value depends on the path taken during the process, so it can vary for different paths between the same initial and final states.
Work (W) is also not a state function. Like heat, its value depends on the path taken during the process, so it can vary for different paths between the same initial and final states.

The quantity that remains unchanged is known as a state function, which depends only on the initial and final states of the system and is independent of the path taken between those states. The only state function among the given options is dQ - dW.dQ is the heat added to the system, and dW is the work done on the system. The first law of thermodynamics states that the change in internal energy of a system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system, i.e., ΔU = dQ - dW. Thus, the quantity dQ - dW is equal to the change in internal energy, which is a state function.
Work (W) is also not a state function. Like heat, its value depends on the path taken during the process, so it can vary for different paths between the same initial and final states.

questions.text: The efficiency of the Carnot engine can never be 1, because:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: We can not achieve the higher temperature
options.explanation: This option is not entirely correct. While it may be challenging to achieve extremely high temperatures in practice, it is not the reason why the efficiency of a Carnot engine cannot be 1. Even if we could achieve the highest possible temperature, the efficiency would still be less than 1 due to the nature of the Carnot cycle.

options.text: We do not have an ideal working substance
options.explanation: While the use of an ideal working substance is an assumption in the Carnot cycle, it is not the reason why the efficiency cannot be 1. Even with an ideal working substance, the efficiency would still be less than 1 due to energy losses in the cycle.

This option correctly identifies energy losses as one of the fundamental reasons why the efficiency of any engine cannot be 1. Energy losses occur due to factors such as friction, heat transfer to the surroundings, and other inefficiencies in the engine's operation. These losses are inherent in real-world engines and prevent the efficiency from reaching 1.

options.text: We need cold resevoir at absolute zero temperature, which is not available
options.explanation: The efficiency of a Carnot engine is defined as the ratio of the work output to the heat input. According to the Carnot cycle, the maximum possible efficiency of an engine operating between two temperatures, T1 (higher temperature) and T2 (lower temperature), is given by the equation:Efficiency = 1 - (T2 / T1)For the efficiency to be 1 (100%), the temperature of the cold reservoir (T2) would have to be absolute zero (0 Kelvin). However, absolute zero temperature is not attainable in practice. Therefore, the efficiency of a Carnot engine can never reach 1.Options A, B, and C are not the correct explanations for why the efficiency of a Carnot engine cannot be 1. Option D provides the correct explanation that the unavailability of a cold reservoir at absolute zero temperature prevents the Carnot engine from achieving an efficiency of 1.Therefore, the correct answer is D).

questions.text: Which of the following is not an example of an adiabatic process?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The rapid escape of air from a burst tyre
options.explanation: The rapid escape of air from a burst tyre: This is an example of an adiabatic process. When a tyre bursts, the rapid release of air occurs without any significant heat exchange with the surroundings.

options.text: The rapid expansion and compression of air through which a sound wave is passing
options.explanation: The rapid expansion and compression of air through which a sound wave is passing: This is also an example of an adiabatic process. As sound waves propagate through air, they cause rapid compression and expansion of air molecules, which occurs without significant heat transfer.

Lifting, also referred to as adiabatic cooling, is the most common method of humidification of air to form clouds. As air rises it expands because pressure decreases with altitude. Kinetic energy is converted to potential energy and the parcel temperature decreases, and the relative humidity increases.

options.text: Slow compression or expansion of gas
options.explanation: Slow compression or expansion of gas: This option is not an example of an adiabatic process. In a slow compression or expansion of gas, there is sufficient time for heat transfer between the gas and its surroundings. Adiabatic processes involve rapid changes where there is no time for significant heat transfer.

questions.text: Internal energy is _of path.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The internal energy of a system is independent of the path taken to reach a particular state. In other words, the internal energy only depends on the initial and final states of the system, regardless of the specific process or pathway used to transition between them. This concept is based on the first law of thermodynamics, which states that the change in internal energy of a system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system.

Dependent: This option suggests that the internal energy of a system depends on the specific path taken to reach a certain state. In this case, the internal energy would not only be influenced by the initial and final states but also by the specific process or pathway used to transition between them. However, this is not the case according to the principles of thermodynamics.

Highly dependent: This option implies that the internal energy of a system is significantly influenced by the path taken. It suggests that the specific process or pathway used to transition between states has a strong impact on the internal energy of the system. However, this is not accurate as the internal energy is path-independent.

Not enough information: This option indicates that there is insufficient information provided to determine whether the internal energy of a system is independent or dependent on the path. Without more context or knowledge, it is difficult to make a definitive conclusion.

questions.text: The energy input to an engine is 4.00 times greater than the work it performs. What fraction of the energy input is expelled to the cold reservoir?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: States that the fraction of the energy input expelled to the cold reservoir is 0.25. However, based on the given information that the energy input is 4.00 times greater than the work performed, the correct fraction is 0.75, not 0.25. Therefore, option (A) is incorrect.

To determine the fraction of the energy input that is expelled to the cold reservoir, we can use the concept of efficiency in thermodynamics. The efficiency (η) of an engine is defined as the ratio of the work output to the energy input. Mathematically, it can be expressed as:η = Work output / Energy inputGiven that the energy input is 4.00 times greater than the work performed, we can write:Energy input = 4 * Work performedSubstituting this into the efficiency equation:η = Work output / (4 * Work performed)We can simplify this equation by canceling out the common factor of work performed:η = 1 / 4So, the efficiency of the engine is 1/4, which means that 1/4 of the energy input is converted into useful work. The remaining fraction (3/4) is expelled to the cold reservoir. Therefore, the correct answer is:B) 0.75Approximately 75% of the energy input is expelled to the cold reservoir.

Option C suggests that the entire energy input is expelled to the cold reservoir, implying an efficiency of 0. This contradicts the given information that the energy input is 4.00 times greater than the work performed. Since there is work being performed, it is not possible for the entire energy input to be expelled. Hence, option C is incorrect.

Option D claims that it is impossible to determine the fraction of the energy input expelled to the cold reservoir. However, based on the given information and the calculation of efficiency, it is possible to determine that the fraction is 0.75. Therefore, option (D) is incorrect.To determine the fraction of the energy input that is expelled to the cold reservoir, we need to consider the efficiency of the engine.

questions.text: The slope of adiabatic (ks) and isothermal (kt) curves related as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: For an ideal gas, the adiabatic bulk modulus Ks is given by:Ks = γP,where P is the pressure of the gas.The isothermal bulk modulus kt is given by:kt = P,where P is the pressure of the gas.Substituting these expressions into the equation above, we get:γP = γktSimplifying, we get:Ks = γktTherefore, the slope of an adiabatic curve and the slope of an isothermal curve for an ideal gas are related by Ks = γkt. This relationship is useful in thermodynamics and fluid dynamics for analyzing the behavior of gasses undergoing adiabatic and isothermal processes.

This relationship is useful in thermodynamics and fluid dynamics for analyzing the behavior of gasses undergoing adiabatic and isothermal processes.

questions.text: An ideal gas at 15.5C and a pressure of 1.72 x 105 Pa occupies a volume of 2.81 m3. If the volume is raised to 4.16 m3 and the temperature raised to 28.2C, what will be the pressure of the gas?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests a pressure that is significantly higher than the initial pressure of the gas. However, when the volume increases, and the temperature rises, according to the ideal gas law, the pressure is expected to decrease. Therefore, this option does not accurately represent the expected pressure of the gas after the change in volume and temperature.

To solve this problem, we can use the combined gas law:(P1V1)/T1 = (P2V2)/T2where P1, V1, and T1 are the initial pressure, volume, and temperature, respectively, and P2, V2, and T2 are the final pressure, volume, and temperature, respectively.Substituting the given values, we get:(1.72 × 10^5 Pa)(2.81 m^3)/(15.5 + 273.15 K) = (P2)(4.16 m^3)/(28.2 + 273.15 K)Solving for P2, we get:P2 = [(1.72 × 10^5 Pa)(2.81 m^3)/(15.5 + 273.15 K)] × [(28.2 + 273.15 K)/(4.16 m^3)]= 1.06 × 10^5 PaTherefore, the pressure of the gas is approximately 1.06 × 10^5 Pa when the volume is raised to 4.16 m^3 and the temperature is raised to 28.2 °C.

However, after the change in volume and temperature, the pressure of the gas is expected to decrease but not to such a low value. questions.text: Heat reservoir has a _ temperature;
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A heat reservoir does not have both high and low temperatures simultaneously. It typically has a fixed temperature and can either supply heat to a system at a lower temperature or absorb heat from a system at a higher temperature, maintaining its own temperature constant in the process.

A heat reservoir refers to a system or body that is capable of providing or absorbing an infinite amount of heat without undergoing a change in temperature. It is characterized by having a constant temperature, regardless of the amount of heat transferred to or from it. Heat reservoirs are often used in thermodynamic processes as a reference or source/sink of heat. They serve as idealized models to simplify calculations and analysis of heat transfer.

This option is incorrect. A heat reservoir does not have a variable temperature. It is specifically designed or chosen to maintain a constant temperature, ensuring that it can supply or absorb heat at a consistent rate without any fluctuations in temperature.

This option suggests that the temperature of a heat reservoir could be zero. However, it's important to note that a heat reservoir is typically chosen to have a temperature significantly different from zero to facilitate heat transfer processes effectively. Nonetheless, the defining characteristic of a heat reservoir is its constant temperature, regardless of whether it is high or low.

This option focuses on the temperature being high but doesn't fully capture the defining characteristic of a heat reservoir, which is its constant temperature. While heat reservoirs are often chosen to have temperatures higher than the systems they interact with, it's the constancy of temperature that is the key feature.

questions.text: A monatomic ideal gas is thermally insulated, so no heat can flow between it and its surroundings. Is it possible for the temperature of the gas to rise?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Yes. The temperature can rise if work is done by the gas.
options.explanation: This option suggests that the temperature of the gas can rise if work is done by the gas. According to the first law of thermodynamics, the change in internal energy (ΔU) of a system is equal to the heat added to the system (Q) minus the work done by the system (W): ΔU=Q−W. If work is done by the gas (W is positive), the internal energy of the gas decreases, which could result in a decrease in temperature.

options.text: No. The only way that the temperature can rise is if heat is added to the gas.
options.explanation: This option suggests that the only way for the temperature of the gas to rise is by adding heat to the gas. This statement is generally correct. According to the ideal gas law, when heat is added to a gas at constant volume, its temperature increases. However, if the gas is thermally insulated, no heat can flow into or out of the gas, so the temperature cannot increase.

options.text: Yes. The temperature can rise if the work is done on the gas.
options.explanation: In a thermally insulated system, where no heat can flow between the gas and its surroundings, the change in internal energy of the gas is solely determined by the work done on or by the gas and not by heat transfer.If work is done on the gas, such as compressing the gas or applying external forces to it, the gas can gain energy. This increase in energy corresponds to an increase in temperature. The work done on the gas increases the kinetic energy of its molecules, leading to a rise in temperature.

options.text: No. The only way that the temperature can rise is by adding more molecules in container.
options.explanation: This option suggests that the only way for the temperature of the gas to rise is by adding more molecules to the container. While adding more molecules to a gas can increase its temperature under certain conditions (such as constant volume), in the scenario described (thermally insulated), the number of molecules in the container remains constant. Therefore, this option is not applicable in this context.

questions.explanation: In a thermally insulated system, where no heat can flow between the gas and its surroundings, the change in internal energy of the gas is solely determined by the work done on or by the gas and not by heat transfer.If work is done on the gas, such as compressing the gas or applying external forces to it, the gas can gain energy. This increase in energy corresponds to an increase in temperature. The work done on the gas increases the kinetic energy of its molecules, leading to a rise in temperature.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the change in internal energy is negative, indicating a decrease in internal energy. However, according to the first law of thermodynamics (ΔU=Q−W), the change in internal energy is determined by the net effect of heat absorbed (Q) and work done (W) on the system. If more heat is absorbed than work done, the internal energy increases, resulting in a positive change. In this case, 10 J of heat is absorbed, and 5 J of work is done, resulting in a net increase in internal energy, not a decrease. Therefore, option A) is incorrect.

This option suggests that the change in internal energy is equal to the heat absorbed, neglecting the effect of work done. However, according to the first law of thermodynamics (ΔU=Q−W), the change in internal energy depends on both heat absorbed (Q) and work done (W). In this case, 5 J of work is done on the system, which affects the net change in internal energy. Therefore, option B) is incorrect.

This option suggests that the change in internal energy is the sum of the heat absorbed and the work done. However, according to the first law of thermodynamics (ΔU=Q−W), the change in internal energy is determined by the net effect of heat absorbed (Q) and work done (W) on the system. Adding heat absorbed and work done together without considering their opposing effects does not accurately represent the change in internal energy. Therefore, option C) is incorrect.

This option correctly represents the change in internal energy (ΔU) calculated using the first law of thermodynamics (ΔU=Q−W). It acknowledges both the heat absorbed (Q=10J) and the work done (W=5J) on the system. The positive value indicates that the internal energy of the system increases due to the net input of energy (10 J of heat absorbed minus 5 J of work done). Therefore, option D) is the correct answer, providing a proper explanation of the change in internal energy.

questions.explanation: According to the first law of thermodynamics, the change in internal energy (∆U) of a system is equal to the heat absorbed by the system (∆Q) minus the work done by the system (∆W):∆U = ∆Q - ∆WSubstituting the given values, we get:∆U = 10 J - 5 J∆U = 5 JTherefore, the change in internal energy is 5 J, and the correct answer is option D.

questions.text: In which process is the net work done zero?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The net work done in a cyclic process is zero if the system returns to its initial state at the end of the process.

In free expansion, a gas expands into a vacuum or an environment with significantly lower pressure without any external constraints. Since there is no external pressure or resistance, no work is done on or by the gas during the expansion. Therefore, the net work done in free expansion is zero.
The net work done in an isobaric process is zero if the pressure is constant throughout the process.
The net work done in an adiabatic process is zero if there is no heat transfer between the system and the surroundings.

questions.explanation: The net work done is zero in a free expansion process.In a free expansion, the gas expands into a vacuum. There is no external pressure opposing the expansion, so the gas does no work. The work done by a gas is equal to the pressure multiplied by the change in volume. Since there is no external pressure in a free expansion, the work done is zero.

questions.text: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of the gas is found to be proportional to the fourth power of temperature. The ideal gas would be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Hydrogen is a diatomic gas. For diatomic gases, the adiabatic index γ is approximately 7557 or5335, depending on the rotational degrees of freedom. None of these values match γ=34, so hydrogen is not the correct answer.
Helium is a monatomic gas. For monatomic gases, such as helium, the adiabatic index γ is approximately 5/3. This value does not match y=4/3, so helium is not the correct answer.

.Methane is a hydrocarbon and not a monatomic or diatomic gas. Its behavior would be more complex and does not follow the simple ideal gas law relationship suggested in the question. Therefore, it is not the correct answer.

Considering the characteristics of hydrogen and helium, neither gas individually matches the condition where y=4/3. However, it is possible that a mixture of gases could exhibit such behavior. By combining the properties of both gases in a suitable proportion, it might be possible to achieve an adiabatic index y=4/3. Therefore, a mixture of hydrogen and helium could potentially be the correct answer.

questions.explanation: From the given condition, we have:P = α * T4Using the ideal gas law, PV = nRT, we can express the temperature in terms of pressure and volume:T = (PV) / (nR)Substituting this into the condition, we have:P = α * [(PV) / (nR)]4Simplifying, we get:P * V(4/3) = 1 / [(α * β)3]Comparing this with the equation for an adiabatic process, PVγ = const., we can determine the value of γ:γ = 4/3The adiabatic constant γ is related to the degrees of freedom (f) of the gas molecules:γ = 1 + 2/fSetting γ = 4/3 and solving for f, we find:4/3 = 1 + 2/f2/f = 1/3f = 6A gas with 6 degrees of freedom corresponds to CH2, which is the correct answer. Therefore, option C is the correct choice.

questions.text: If 1 mole of an ideal gas is heated at constant volume so that its temperature rises by delta T, then first law of thermodynamics can be written as:

options.explanation: This option suggests that the change in internal energy (ΔU) plus the specific heat at constant volume (Cv ) equals the temperature change (ΔT). However, this equation does not accurately represent the first law of thermodynamics. In the first law, the change in internal energy is directly proportional to the heat added to the system at constant volume (Q=ΔU), not the sum of the specific heat and the change in internal energy. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

This option suggests that the change in internal energy (ΔU) is equal to the specific heat at constant volume (Cv ) minus the change in temperature (ΔT). However, this equation does not accurately represent the relationship between the change in internal energy, specific heat, and temperature in the first law of thermodynamics. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

ΔW=0Using the first law of thermodynamics equation:ΔQ=ΔU+ΔWΔQ=ΔU eq (1)The heat given is Q,which is used to provide the Molar specific heat at constant volume which is given as CvΔT.Eq (1) becomes ΔU=CvΔTHence, C is the correct option.

questions.text: The internal energy of the system depends on?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Initial and final states of the system and the path from the initial to final state
options.explanation: This option suggests that the internal energy of the system depends not only on the initial and final states but also on the path taken from the initial to the final state. However, according to the first law of thermodynamics, internal energy is a state function, meaning it depends only on the state of the system (its properties like temperature, pressure, etc.) and not on the path taken to reach that state. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Initial and final states of the system only
options.explanation: Internal energy is a state of function therefore, change in internal energy of system depends only upon "the initial and final states of the system" .

options.text: Initial state of the system and the path from the initial to final state
options.explanation: This option suggests that the internal energy depends on the initial state of the system and the path taken from the initial to the final state. However, as mentioned earlier, internal energy is a state function, and it does not depend on the path taken but only on the initial and final states of the system. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: The path from the initial to final state
options.explanation: This option suggests that the internal energy depends only on the path taken from the initial to the final state. However, as previously explained, internal energy is a state function, and it does not depend on the path taken but only on the initial and final states of the system. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Internal energy is a state of function therefore, change in internal energy of system depends only upon "the initial and final states of the system" .

options.explanation: For Specific heat capacity, use the formula Q = mcΔT.And for the latent heat of fusion of ice, use the formula Q = ML.The heat that is taken by the ice for it to melt is equal to the heat lost by the water.Energy gained by ice = energy lost by the water.ML = mcΔT.ML = mc(290K - 273K).ML = mc x 17.Then we divide through by L.M = 17 mc/L.Please note that the 273K that we have used in this solution is the temperature at which water freezes.Therefore the minimum mass of required ice is = "17 mc/L".

questions.text: Which of the following is the statement of the first law of thermodynamics?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Difference between the heat given to the system and work done by the system appears as internal energy of the system
options.explanation: The First Law of Thermodynamics states that the internal energy of the system is equal to sum of heat given to the system and work done by the system. This means that heat energy cannot be created or destroyed.

options.text: Difference between work done and the change in internal energy is equal to the heat absorbed by the system
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The first law of thermodynamics, also known as the law of energy conservation, states that the change in internal energy of a closed system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system. In other words, the increase in the internal energy of a system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system.

options.text: Some of the heat absorbed and the increase in internal energy is equal to the work done by the system
options.explanation: This statement is not a correct expression of the first law of thermodynamics. The first law states that the increase in the internal energy of a system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system, not "some of the heat absorbed."

options.text: Some of the heat absorbed and the work done by the system is equal to the decrease in the internal energy of the system
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The first law of thermodynamics states that the change in the internal energy of a closed system is equal to the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system. It does not involve a decrease in internal energy.

questions.explanation: The First Law of Thermodynamics states that the internal energy of the system is equal to sum of heat given to the system and work done by the system. This means that heat energy cannot be created or destroyed.

questions.text: The flow of heat energy from the hot to the cold body is a/an _ process.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A reversible process would imply that the flow of heat energy can be reversed without any loss or dissipation of energy. In a reversible process, the hot body and the cold body could exchange heat back and forth without any net change in the overall system. However, this is not the case in reality, as some energy is always lost as heat during any actual heat transfer process.

The correct option is "irreversible process." Heat energy naturally flows from a hot body to a cold body in an irreversible process. This is due to the second law of thermodynamics, which states that heat energy tends to disperse or spread out over time. In an irreversible process, the flow of heat is spontaneous and unidirectional, meaning it cannot be easily reversed or undone.

The option "depends on the conditions" is incorrect because the flow of heat energy from hot to cold is universally observed regardless of specific conditions. The direction of heat flow is determined solely by the temperature difference between the two bodies involved.

The option "conduction process" is incorrect because it refers to a specific mechanism of heat transfer, which is one of the three primary modes of heat transfer alongside convection and radiation. While conduction can occur during the heat transfer process, the question is asking about the overall nature of the process, not the specific mechanism involved.

questions.explanation: In heat conduction, heat is transferred from one place to another. This process is not reversible. Heat can be transferred through radiation also. It is also irreversible.

questions.text: Which one of the following is irreversible?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Slow compression of an elastic spring
options.explanation: "Slow compression of an elastic spring" is a reversible process. When you slowly compress an elastic spring, you can reverse the process by slowly releasing the compression, and the spring will return to its original state. The elasticity of the spring allows it to store potential energy when compressed and release it when uncompressed.

options.text: Slow evaporation of a substance in an isolated vessel
options.explanation: "Slow evaporation of a substance in an isolated vessel" is a reversible process. In an isolated vessel, the evaporated substance can be condensed back into its liquid state by cooling the vapor, returning it to its original state. The process is reversible because it can be undone without any net effect on the system.

"Slow compression of a gas" is generally a reversible process. If the compression is slow enough and carried out without significant energy losses, the process can be reversed by slowly expanding the gas back to its original volume, returning it to its initial state. However, if the compression is rapid or accompanied by significant energy losses (e.g., friction or heat transfer to the surroundings), the process may become irreversible.

"A chemical explosion" is an irreversible process. A chemical explosion involves a rapid release of energy, typically through a highly exothermic reaction. Once an explosion occurs, it cannot be reversed to bring the reactants back to their original state. The energy released during the explosion dissipates into the surroundings, and the system irreversibly changes.

questions.explanation: The chemical changes are irreversible. The reactants form products that can be changed back into their original form. The reactants form products that can never be changed back to their original form Options A,B&C are reversible.

questions.text: A fixed mass of gas undergoes isochoric (isovolumetric) change. As a result, the pressure of the gas has increased. Which of the following is true about the gas?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Internal energy = decrease direction of kinetic energy = heat released from the gas
options.explanation: This option suggests that the internal energy of the gas decreases, implying that the gas loses energy. However, in an isochoric process where pressure increases, energy is typically added to the gas in the form of heat, resulting in an increase in internal energy.Additionally, the direction of kinetic energy is mentioned to be towards heat released from the gas. This is inconsistent because in thermodynamic processes, heat is either absorbed or released by the system, affecting its internal energy, but the direction of kinetic energy is not explicitly related to heat transfer.Overall, this option is incorrect because it misinterprets the change in internal energy and incorrectly associates kinetic energy with heat transfer.

options.text: Internal energy = decrease direction of transfer of thermal energy = heat absorbed by the gas
options.explanation: This option suggests that the internal energy of the gas decreases, which contradicts the typical behavior of gases in an isochoric process where energy is added to the system.Additionally, it states that the direction of transfer of thermal energy is towards heat absorbed by the gas. However, in an isochoric process, the gas typically absorbs heat to increase its internal energy, not release it.Therefore, this option is incorrect because it misinterprets the change in internal energy and incorrectly identifies the direction of heat transfer.

options.text: Internal energy = increase direction of transfer of thermal energy = heat released by the gas
options.explanation: This option correctly identifies that the internal energy of the gas increases, which aligns with the typical behavior of gases in an isochoric process where energy is added to the system.However, it incorrectly states that the direction of transfer of thermal energy is towards heat released by the gas. In reality, in an isochoric process, the gas typically absorbs heat to increase its internal energy.Therefore, while partially correct in identifying the change in internal energy, this option is ultimately incorrect due to its misinterpretation of the direction of heat transfer.

options.text: Internal energy = increase direction of transfer of thermal energy= heat absorbed by the gas
options.explanation: In isovolumetric change, volume doesn't change so no work can be done so heat can only be transferred in the form of thermal energy and heat must be absorbed by the gas so Option D will be the correct answer.

questions.explanation: In isovolumetric change, volume doesn't change so no work can be done so heat can only be transferred in the form of thermal energy and heat must be absorbed by the gas so Option D will be the correct answer.

This option suggests that the temperatures of the heat source and the heat sink are 99°C and 25°C, respectively.However, based on our calculations, the temperature of the heat source (Tℎ ) is found to be 372 K, which is approximately 99°C, and the temperature of the heat sink (Tc ) is 310 K, which is not equivalent to 25°C.Therefore, option C) does not match the calculated temperatures and is incorrect.

Efficiency = T2-T1/T2 = ⅙ By rearranging we can get 6T2-6T1 = T2 T2 = 1.2T1T2 is the source and T1 is the sink. When the sink temperature is reduced by 62, the efficiency gets doubled. T2-(T1-62)/T2 = 2 x ⅙ = 2/6 By rearranging this equation we get 6T2-6T1+372 = 2T2 4T2 - 6T1 + 372 =0 Substitute the value of T2 from the first equation 4(1.2T1)-6T1+372 1.2T1 = 372 = 310K = 37˚C T2= 1.2 x 310 = 372K = 99˚C

questions.text: Under which conditions does a real gas approximate to an ideal gas?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At high pressure and high density, the volume occupied by the gas molecules becomes significant compared to the overall volume of the gas.Under these conditions, intermolecular forces between gas molecules become more pronounced, leading to deviations from ideal gas behavior.Real gases tend to deviate more from ideal behavior at high pressures and densities.

At low pressure and high density, the volume occupied by the gas molecules is relatively small compared to the overall volume of the gas.While the density is high, the molecules are still relatively close together, increasing the likelihood of intermolecular interactions.However, at low pressures, the effect of intermolecular forces is typically less pronounced, and the gas may behave more like an ideal gas compared to high-pressure conditions.

At high pressure and low density, the gas molecules are relatively far apart, and intermolecular forces may have less influence on the gas behavior.However, at high pressures, the volume occupied by the gas becomes significant, and the molecules may start to experience closer interactions, leading to deviations from ideal behavior.Therefore, the combination of high pressure and low density may not favor ideal gas behavior.

Real gases approximate ideal gas behavior at relatively low density, low pressure, and high temperature.At high temperatures, the gas molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular forces, but at low temperatures, the gas has less kinetic energy and thus the intermolecular forces are more prominent. At low gas densities and low pressures, there is lots of space between molecules, but at high densities, the molecules are crowded and thus the volume of the gas particles is no longer negligible. Lower pressures mean fewer interactions and those interactions can be more easily thought of as elastic collisions.Hence, D is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Real gases approximate ideal gas behavior at relatively low density, low pressure, and high temperature.At high temperatures, the gas molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular forces, but at low temperatures, the gas has less kinetic energy and thus the intermolecular forces are more prominent. At low gas densities and low pressures, there is lots of space between molecules, but at high densities, the molecules are crowded and thus the volume of the gas particles is no longer negligible. Lower pressures mean fewer interactions and those interactions can be more easily thought of as elastic collisions.Hence, D is the correct option.

questions.text: A monatomic gas is heated from temperature T1 and T2 under two different conditions at (i) constant volume and (ii) constant pressure. The change in U is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Since at constant volume, all the supplied heat energy increases the internal energy of the gas, ΔU would be proportional to the change in temperature (ΔT) only.

At constant pressure, in addition to the change in temperature (ΔT), there's also work done (PΔV) contributing to the change in internal energy. Therefore, the change in internal energy (ΔU) would be more significant for this process.

Change in internal energy will be ΔU = nCvΔT for both process (at constant volume and constant pressure). It doesn't depend upon the type of process. It is a state function, so the change in temperature is the same in both cases hence △U is same in both.Hence, C is the correct option.

options.text: Independent of the number of moles
options.explanation: The change in internal energy (ΔU) does depend on the number of moles (n) of the gas, as indicated by the n term in the formulas for ΔU.

questions.explanation: Change in internal energy will be ΔU = nCvΔT for both process (at constant volume and constant pressure). It doesn't depend upon the type of process. It is a state function, so the change in temperature is the same in both cases hence △U is same in both.Hence, C is the correct option.

questions.text: The efficiency of diesel engines is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option (a) "10% to 20%" is a range of efficiency percentages. While this range is not entirely accurate for modern diesel engines, historically, some older or less efficient diesel engines may have had efficiencies in this range.

A typical diesel automotive engine operates at around 30% to 35% of thermal efficiency. About 65-70% is rejected as waste heat without being converted into useful work, i.e., work delivered to wheels.In general, engines using the Diesel cycle are usually more efficient than engines using the Otto cycle.
Option (c) "35% to 40%" is also a possible range of efficiency percentages for certain advanced and well-optimized diesel engines. This range is commonly seen in standard diesel engines.

"40% to 50%" represents a high range of efficiency percentages. While some highly efficient diesel engines in specific applications (e.g., marine or power generation) may achieve efficiencies in this range, it is generally not the average efficiency for most diesel engines.

questions.text: Two objects, with different sizes, masses, and temperatures, are placed in thermal contact. In which direction does the energy travel?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Energy travels from the larger object to the smaller object
options.explanation: This option suggests that energy flows from the larger object to the smaller object.However, the direction of energy transfer does not solely depend on the size of the objects. Other factors such as temperature difference and thermal conductivity also play significant roles.Therefore, option A is not a comprehensive explanation of the direction of energy transfer.

options.text: Energy travels from the object with more mass to the one with less mass
options.explanation: The direction of energy transfer is not solely determined by the mass of the objects. While mass can affect the rate of temperature change, it is not the sole determinant of the direction of energy transfer.Energy transfer primarily occurs from the object at a higher temperature to the one at a lower temperature, regardless of mass.Therefore, option B is not a correct explanation of the direction of energy transfer.

options.text: Energy travels from the object at higher temperature to the object at lower temperature
options.explanation: If two objects at different temperatures are brought in contact with each other, energy is transferred from the hotter object (that is, the object with the greater temperature) to the colder (lower temperature) object, until both objects are at the same temperature .

This option contradicts the fundamental principles of thermodynamics, which state that energy transfer occurs in response to a temperature gradient.Energy always flows from regions of higher temperature to regions of lower temperature until thermal equilibrium is achieved.Therefore, option D is not a correct explanation of the direction of energy transfer.

questions.explanation: If two objects at different temperatures are brought in contact with each other, energy is transferred from the hotter object (that is, the object with the greater temperature) to the colder (lower temperature) object, until both objects are at the same temperature .

questions.text: A 300 W heater is used to boil 500g of water at l00 °C. How long should the heater be switched on? (The specific latent heat of vaporization of water is 2230 J/g).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To determine the time required to boil the water, we can use the formula:Energy = Power x TimeGiven:Power of the heater (P) = 300WMass of water (m) = 500gSpecific latent heat of vaporization of water (L) = 2230 J/gFirst, we need to calculate the energy required to vaporize the given mass of water.Energy = Mass x Specific latent heatEnergy = 500g x 2230 J/gEnergy = 1,115,000 JNow, we can use the formula to calculate the time required:Energy = Power x Time1,115,000 J = 300W x TimeRearranging the equation to solve for timeTime = Energy / PowerTime = 1,115,000J / 300WTime = 3716.67 secondsConverting the time to minutesTime (in minutes) = 3716.67 seconds / 60 seconds/minuteTime (in minutes) ≈ 62 minutesTherefore, the correct answer is Option A: 62 minutes.Other options are incorrect

The specific latent heat of vaporization represents the energy required to convert a given amount of substance from a liquid to a gas at its boiling point. Therefore, Option C is incorrect.

questions.explanation: t = Q/P= m x lf/P= 500 x 2230/300 = 3717 seconds = 62 minutes

questions.text: The energy input to an engine is four times greater than the work it performs. What is its thermal efficiency?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it suggests that the thermal efficiency is 4.00. However, the thermal efficiency is a ratio expressed as a percentage, so it should be a value between 0 and 100. It cannot be 4:00.

This option is incorrect because it suggests that the thermal efficiency is 1.00. However, a thermal efficiency of 1.00 implies 100%, meaning that all the energy input is converted into work output, which contradicts the given information that the energy input is four times greater than the work output.

To determine the thermal efficiency of an engine, we need to calculate the ratio of the work output to the energy input.Thermal Efficiency (η) is defined as:η = (Work output / Energy input) * 100%Given that the energy input of the engine is 4.00 times greater than the work it performs, we can express this relationship as:Energy input = 4 * Work outputSubstituting this into the thermal efficiency formula:η = (Work output / (4 * Work output)) * 100%η = 1/4 * 100%η = 25%Therefore, the thermal efficiency is 25%, which is equivalent to 0.25.This means that the engine is able to convert only 25% of the energy input into useful work output, while the remaining 75% is lost as waste heat or in other inefficiencies.

This option is incorrect because, with the given information, it is indeed possible to determine thermal efficiency by applying the formula. We can calculate thermal efficiency based on the ratio of work output to energy input.

questions.text: Two containers, X and Y, are filled with an ideal gas. X has 1 mol of gas and Y has 2 mol of gas. The volume of Y is four times that of X. Pressure in Y is half that in X. What is the temperature of the gas in Y relative to the temperature of the gas in X?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the gas in container Y were at twice the temperature of the gas in container X, it would mean T2 = 2T. However, we cannot determine this based on the given information, so option A is incorrect.

The temperature of the gas in container Y is equal to the temperature of the gas in container X.According to the ideal gas law, the pressure (P), volume (V), and number of moles (n) of an ideal gas are related to its temperature (T) by the equation PV = nRT, where R is the ideal gas constant.In this scenario, we have two containers, X and Y. Container X contains 1 mol of gas, and container Y contains 2 mol of gas. The volume of container Y is four times that of container X. We are given that the pressure in Y is half that in X.Let's denote the pressure in container X as P_X and the pressure in container Y as P_Y. Since P_Y is half of P_X, we can express it as P_Y = 0.5 P_X.Since the number of moles and volume are different between the two containers, the only way for the equation PV = nRT to hold true for both containers is if the temperature (T) is the same for both containers. Therefore, the temperature of the gas in container Y is equal to the temperature of the gas in container X.Hence, the correct answer is "Equal."
Without any further information, we cannot conclude that the temperature of the gas in container Y is equal to the temperature of the gas in container X. Option C is incorrect.

This option suggests that the temperature of the gas in container Y could be between 1 and 4 times the temperature of the gas in container X. However, we cannot determine the exact relationship without additional information, so option D is incorrect.

questions.text: The value of the triple point of water is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The triple point of water is not at 1K. The triple point is a specific temperature at which water can exist in all three phases simultaneously: solid (ice), liquid (water), and gas (water vapor). The triple point is precisely defined and accepted to be slightly above 0°C.
The triple point of water is not at 100K either. 100K is a significantly lower temperature than the actual triple point of water.

The triple point of water is defined to be exactly 273.16K (0.01°C or 32.018°F). At this temperature and under a specific pressure of 611.657 pascals (0.00604 atmospheres), water can exist simultaneously as ice, liquid water, and water vapor.The value of the triple point of pure water is 0.01 °C (273.16K, 32.01°F)

While 0K (absolute zero) is the lowest possible temperature, it is not the value of the triple point of water. At absolute zero, water cannot exist in its liquid or gaseous states, and the triple point cannot occur.

questions.explanation: The triple point of pure water is at 0.01°C (273.16K, 32.01°F)

questions.text: When the temperature of the system does not change, it is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: "The process in which the temperature of a system remains constant during the change is called an isothermal process."
"The process in which there is no exchange of heat between the system and its surroundings is called an adiabatic process."
"The process in which the volume of a system remains constant during the change is called an isovolumetric (or isometric) process."
"The process in which the pressure of a system remains constant during the change is called an isobaric process."

questions.explanation: Isothermal refers to a process in which a system changes—whether it be the pressure, volume, or contents—without the temperature changing.

questions.text: A gas expands from V1 to V2. The amount of work done is greatest in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an adiabatic process, there is no heat exchange between the system and its surroundings (Q=0).The work done in an adiabatic process is determined by the change in internal energy (W=ΔU).While adiabatic processes can involve significant changes in internal energy, the work done may not necessarily be the greatest compared to other processes.

We plot P-V graph for same changes for these processesNow, from the graph we can deduce the work done by each process by calculating area under the curve for work done and it is evident that greatest area under the curve is for Isobaric process then Isothermal process and the Adiabatic process .Hence , Work Done willIsobaric > Isothermal > Adiabatic

In an isochoric process, the volume remains constant (V=constant).Since there is no change in volume (ΔV=0), the work done in an isochoric process is zero (W=0).Therefore, no work is done in an isochoric process, making it unlikely to have the greatest amount of work done.

In an isothermal process, the temperature remains constant (T=constant).The work done in an isothermal process depends on the volume change.While work can indeed be done in an isothermal process, it may not necessarily be the greatest compared to other processes if the volume change is relatively small.

questions.explanation: We plot P-V graph for same changes for these processesNow, from the graph we can deduce the work done by each process by calculating area under the curve for work done and it is evident that greatest area under the curve is for Isobaric process then Isothermal process and the Adiabatic process .Hence , Work Done willIsobaric > Isothermal > Adiabatic

questions.text: A fraction of internal energy is due to molecular vibration, which is different in different states of matter. Which of the following gives the correct order of fraction of internal energy due to molecular vibration?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Solids have the greatest fraction of internal energy due to molecular vibration, followed by liquids and then gases. questions.text: In thermodynamics, zeroth law is related to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The zeroth law of thermodynamics is not directly related to work. It is related to thermal equilibrium and the concept of temperature. However, work is an important concept in thermodynamics and is related to the first and second laws of thermodynamics.

The zeroth law of thermodynamics is not directly related to energy. It is related to thermal equilibrium and the concept of temperature. However, energy is an important concept in thermodynamics and is related to the first and second laws of thermodynamics.

The zeroth law of thermodynamics is related to thermal equilibrium. This law establishes the concept of temperature and allows us to define a temperature scale. The law states that if two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

The zeroth law of thermodynamics is not directly related to entropy. It is related to thermal equilibrium and the concept of temperature. However, entropy is an important concept in thermodynamics and is related to the second law of thermodynamics.

questions.text: Under a cyclic path, internal energy after the complete cycle is the same as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If a cyclic process is carried out on a system, the internal energy of the system will be the same at the end of the process as it was at the beginning of the process. If the cyclic process is reversible, then the internal energy of the system will be the same at the end of the process as it was at the beginning of the process. If the cyclic process is irreversible, then the internal energy of the system may not be the same at the end of the process as it was at the beginning of the process. However, the internal energy of a system will not be twice the initial value after a complete cycle.

The internal energy of a system is a state function, which means that it depends only on the initial and final states of the system and not on the path taken between those states. For a cyclic process, the system returns to its initial state, so the change in internal energy is zero. This means that the internal energy of the system after a complete cycle is the same as the internal energy of the system at the beginning of the cycle. It is not necessarily equal to the internal heat added to the system during the cycle.

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the internal energy of a system is conserved. This means that the internal energy of a system will be the same at the end of a complete cycle as it was initially, at the beginning of the cycle.

For a cyclic process, the net work done by the system is zero, so the work done by the system over a complete cycle is equal in magnitude but opposite in sign to the work done on the system during the reverse cycle. This means that the net work done by the system over a complete cycle is zero. So, initial work is not the same as internal energy.

questions.explanation: According to the first law of thermodynamics, the internal energy of a system is conserved. This means that the internal energy of a system will be the same at the end of a complete cycle as it was initially, at the beginning of the cycle.

questions.text: A 500 W electric heater is used to heat 1 kg of water without energy losses. The specific heat capacity of water is 4.2 kJ/kg K . What is the time taken to heat the water from 25 °C to 75 °C?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The formula for the amount of heat required to heat a substance is:Q = m × c × ΔTwhere Q is the amount of heat, m is the mass of the substance, c is the specific heat capacity, and ΔT is the temperature change.In this case, we have:m = 1 kgc = 4.2 kJ/kg KΔT = 75°C - 25°C = 50°CSo the amount of heat required to heat 1 kg of water from 25°C to 75°C is:Q = m × c ×ΔTQ = 1 kg * 4.2 kJ/kg K * 50°CQ = 210 kJThe formula for power is:P = Q / twhere P is the power, Q is the amount of heat, and t is the time.In this case, we have:P = 500 WQ = 210 kJSo the time taken to heat the water from 25°C to 75°C is:t = Q / Pt = 210 kJ / 500 Wt = 420 secondsTherefore, the time taken to heat the water from 25°C to 75°C is 420 seconds or 7 minutes.

questions.text: The efficiency of the heat engine can be increased by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Decreasing the temperature of high temperature reservoir
options.explanation: Decreasing the temperature of the high temperature reservoir of a heat engine will decrease its efficiency. This is because the efficiency of a heat engine is directly proportional to the difference between the temperature of the high-temperaturehigh-temperature reservoir and the temperature of the low-temperature reservoir. Therefore, decreasing the temperature of the high-temperature reservoir while keeping the temperature of the low-temperature reservoir constant can decrease the efficiency of the heat engine.

options.text: Increasing the temperature of high temperature reservoir
options.explanation: Increasing the temperature of the high temperature reservoir of a heat engine can increase its efficiency. This is because the efficiency of a heat engine is directly proportional to the difference between the temperature of the high temperature reservoir and the temperature of the low temperature reservoir. Therefore, increasing the temperature of the high temperature reservoir while keeping the temperature of the low temperature reservoir constant can increase the efficiency of the heat engine.

options.text: Increasing the temperature of low temperature reservoir
options.explanation: Increasing the temperature of the low temperature reservoir of a heat engine will decrease its efficiency. This is because the efficiency of a heat engine is directly proportional to the difference between the temperature of the high temperature reservoir and the temperature of the low temperature reservoir. Therefore, increasing the temperature of the low temperature reservoir while keeping the temperature of the high temperature reservoir constant can decrease the efficiency of the heat engine.

options.text: Increasing the temperature of both high temperature reservoir and low temperature reservoir
options.explanation: Increasing the temperature of both the high temperature reservoir and the low temperature reservoir of a heat engine can increase its efficiency, but only up to a certain point. This is because the efficiency of a heat engine is directly proportional to the difference between the temperature of the high temperature reservoir and the temperature of the low temperature reservoir. If the temperatures of both reservoirs are increased, the difference between them will decrease, which will decrease the efficiency of the heat engine. Therefore, increasing the temperature of both reservoirs can increase the efficiency of the heat engine, but only up to a certain limit.

questions.explanation: The efficiency of a Carnot engine depends on the temperature of hot and cold reservoirs.The larger the temperature difference between the two reservoirs, the greater the efficiency. So, the efficiency of a heat engine can be increased either by "increasing the temperature of a high-temperature reservoir" or decreasing the temperature of a low-temperature reservoir.

questions.text: 100 W heater is switched on for 5 minutes to melt ice. What is the mass of ice that melts at 0oC? ( The specific latent heat of fusion of ice is 334 J/g).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To calculate the mass of ice that melts, we can use the formula:Q = m x Lwhere:Q = heat energy absorbed or released during a phase change (in joules)m = mass of the substance that melts (in grams)L = specific latent heat of fusion (in J/g)Given information:Power of the heater (P) = 100 WTime (t) = 5 minutes = 5 x 60 seconds = 300 secondsSpecific latent heat of fusion of ice (L) = 334 J/gFirst, we need to calculate the total energy supplied by the heater:Energy (Q) = Power (P) x Time (t)Energy (Q) = 100 W * 300 secondsEnergy (Q) = 30,000 joulesNow, we can calculate the mass of ice that melts:m = Q / Lm = 30,000 joules / 334 J/gm ≈ 90 gSo, approximately 90 g of ice will melt when the 100 W heater is switched on for 5 minutes.
According to given data, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: The molar specific heat of a diatomic gas is measured at constant volume and found to be 29.1 J/mol.K. What are the types of energy that are contributing to the molar specific heat?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The gas molecules have kinetic energy due to their linear motion in three-dimensional space. At higher temperatures, vibrational and rotational energies become more significant, while translational energy dominates in gases. The combined consideration of translational, vibrational, and rotational energy is essential for understanding and predicting the thermodynamic behavior of substances in various states.

Diatomic molecules can also rotate about their centre of mass, and this rotational motion contributes to the molar specific heat. At higher temperatures, vibrational and rotational energies become more significant, while translational energy dominates in gases. The combined consideration of translational, vibrational, and rotational energy is essential for understanding and predicting the thermodynamic behavior of substances in various states.

Diatomic molecules can vibrate as well, and this vibrational motion also contributes to the molar specific heat. At higher temperatures, vibrational and rotational energies become more significant, while translational energy dominates in gases. The combined consideration of translational, vibrational, and rotational energy is essential for understanding and predicting the thermodynamic behavior of substances in various states.

The molar specific heat of a diatomic gas includes contributions from three types of energy:1. Translation: The gas molecules have kinetic energy due to their linear motion in three-dimensional space.2. Rotation: Diatomic molecules can also rotate about their centre of mass, and this rotational motion contributes to the molar specific heat.3. Vibrational: Diatomic molecules can vibrate as well, and this vibrational motion also contributes to the molar specific heat.All three types of energy (translation, rotation, and vibrational) are contributing to the molar specific heat of the diatomic gas.

questions.explanation: The total molar specific heat for a diatomic gas includes contributions from both translational and rotational degrees of freedom, while vibrational contributions are typically negligible at room temperature. questions.text: Which of the following statement is not true about heat engine?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: All real engines are less efficient than Carnot engine
options.explanation: This statement is true. The Carnot engine is an ideal, reversible heat engine that operates between two heat reservoirs at different temperatures. It is the most efficient engine possible, and all real engines are less efficient than the Carnot engine due to various losses and inefficiencies.

options.text: All real engines are less efficient due to friction and heat losses
options.explanation: This statement is also true. Real engines experience various inefficiencies, such as friction in moving parts, heat losses to the surroundings, and incomplete combustion in internal combustion engines. These losses reduce the overall efficiency of real engines compared to the ideal Carnot engine.

options.text: Efficiency of Carnot engine working between same two temperatures, depends on the nature of working substance
options.explanation: The statement "Efficiency of the Carnot engine working between two same temperature, depending on the nature of the working substance" is not true about the heat engine.

options.text: The larger the temperature difference of two reservoirs, the greater is the efficiency.
options.explanation: This statement is true. The efficiency of any heat engine, including the Carnot engine, is higher when there is a larger temperature difference (Th - Tc) between the hot and cold reservoirs. A larger temperature difference allows more efficient conversion of heat energy into work, leading to higher efficiency.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct since it’s the only incorrect statement as the efficiency of a 'Carnot engine' depends solely on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs.The efficiency of a Carnot engine, which is the theoretical maximum efficiency for a heat engine operating between two temperature reservoirs, depends only on the temperatures of the reservoirs and is independent of the nature of the working substance. This efficiency is the maximum achievable efficiency for any heat engine operating between the same two temperature reservoirs. The Carnot efficiency is a function of the temperatures only and is not influenced by the specific properties of the working substance. The proof of this result is based on the second law of thermodynamics and relies on the assumption that the Carnot engine is reversible and operates in a cycle.In practice, no real engine can reach the Carnot efficiency because of irreversibilities and losses in actual processes. However, the Carnot efficiency serves as a benchmark for the maximum efficiency that any heat engine can achieve.
